metadata.title: Communications and Digital Committee commonssitc@parliament. uk commonssitc@parliament. uk holcommun
metadata.author: Ofcom

chunks.text: Communications and Digital Committee commonssitc@parliament. uk commonssitc@parliament. uk holcommunications@parliament. uk +44 (0)20 719 2793 +44 (0)20 719 8662 holcommunications@parliament. uk +44 (0)20 719 8662 X: @LordsCommsCom Baroness Maggie Jones Parliamentary Under Secretary of State Minister for the Future Digital Economy and Online Safety Department for Science, Innovation & Technology 21 May 2025 Re. Scrutiny of implementation of the Online Safety ActDear Baroness Jones, We are writing to express our concern about the department’s handling of scrutiny for statutory instruments and other documents related to implementation of the Online Safety Act (OSA). The government has repeatedly committed to facilitating scrutiny of the implementation of the Act. In September 2023, Lord Parkinson of Whitley Bay, the then-Minister, made the commitment that the government would, "where possible, share draft statutory instruments directly with the relevant committees before the formal laying process." On 4 February 2025, Feryal Clark MP, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, stated that the “Science, Innovation and Technology Committee and the Communications and Digital Committee of the House of Lords will play a vital role in scrutinising the regime.” Also in February 2025, you acknowledged the “importance of parliamentary scrutiny of the implementation of the Online Safety Act”, and said that you welcome “the expertise Members of both Houses bring.” However, as noted by members of the Lords, this system "has not been implemented by the Government." Our committees have received documents with the following timescales: • Draft Online Safety Act 2023 (Priority Offences) (Amendment) Regulations 2024 : Received on the afternoon of Wednesday 11 September 2024, before being laid the following day. • Draft Online Safety Act 2023 (Category 1, 2Aand 2B Threshold Conditions) Regulations 2025 : Received after 4pm on Friday 13 December Communications and Digital Committee commonssitc@parliament. uk commonssitc@parliament. uk holcommunications@parliament. uk +44 (0)20 719 2793 +44 (0)20 719 8662 holcommunications@parliament. uk +44 (0)20 719 8662 X: @LordsCommsCom 2024, before being laid on Monday 16 December. • The Protection of Children Code of Practice for Search Services and the Protection of Children Code of Practice for User-to-User Services: We received a letter from you after 3pm on Tuesday 22 April 2025, giving minimal details about the codes, before they were laid on Thursday 24 April. It did not include a copy of the codes themselves. • Draft Statement of Strategic Priorities on Online Safety : We note that we were invited totakepart in a previous consultation on the Statement. Following the consultation, we received a letter from the Secretary of State at 6pm on Tuesday 6 May 2025, in for ming us that the Statement would be laid on Thursday 8 May. It did not include a copy of the Statement. In none of these cases was there opportunity for effective scrutiny by either committee. In your letter dated 25 April 2025, you referred to several OSA statutory instruments that will be subject to parliamentary processes “in the coming months”.

Scrutiny of implementation of the Online Safety ActDear Baroness Jones, We are writing to express our concern about the department’s handling of scrutiny for statutory instruments and other documents related to implementation of the Online Safety Act (OSA). The government has repeatedly committed to facilitating scrutiny of the implementation of the Act. In September 2023, Lord Parkinson of Whitley Bay, the then-Minister, made the commitment that the government would, "where possible, share draft statutory instruments directly with the relevant committees before the formal laying process." On 4 February 2025, Feryal Clark MP, Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Science, Innovation and Technology, stated that the “Science, Innovation and Technology Committee and the Communications and Digital Committee of the House of Lords will play a vital role in scrutinising the regime.” Also in February 2025, you acknowledged the “importance of parliamentary scrutiny of the implementation of the Online Safety Act”, and said that you welcome “the expertise Members of both Houses bring.” However, as noted by members of the Lords, this system "has not been implemented by the Government." Our committees have received documents with the following timescales: • Draft Online Safety Act 2023 (Priority Offences) (Amendment) Regulations 2024 : Received on the afternoon of Wednesday 11 September 2024, before being laid the following day. • Draft Online Safety Act 2023 (Category 1, 2Aand 2B Threshold Conditions) Regulations 2025 : Received after 4pm on Friday 13 December Communications and Digital Committee commonssitc@parliament. uk commonssitc@parliament. uk holcommunications@parliament. uk +44 (0)20 719 2793 +44 (0)20 719 8662 holcommunications@parliament. uk +44 (0)20 719 8662 X: @LordsCommsCom 2024, before being laid on Monday 16 December. • The Protection of Children Code of Practice for Search Services and the Protection of Children Code of Practice for User-to-User Services: We received a letter from you after 3pm on Tuesday 22 April 2025, giving minimal details about the codes, before they were laid on Thursday 24 April. It did not include a copy of the codes themselves. • Draft Statement of Strategic Priorities on Online Safety : We note that we were invited totakepart in a previous consultation on the Statement. Following the consultation, we received a letter from the Secretary of State at 6pm on Tuesday 6 May 2025, in for ming us that the Statement would be laid on Thursday 8 May. It did not include a copy of the Statement. In none of these cases was there opportunity for effective scrutiny by either committee. In your letter dated 25 April 2025, you referred to several OSA statutory instruments that will be subject to parliamentary processes “in the coming months”.

chunks.text: 8 May. It did not include a copy of the Statement. In none of these cases was there opportunity for effective scrutiny by either committee. In your letter dated 25 April 2025, you referred to several OSA statutory instruments that will be subject to parliamentary processes “in the coming months”. Notification of forthcoming OSA secondary legislation, and the parliamentary procedures it will be subject to, is welcome. However, notification alone, without a clear timeline, does not enable our committees to factor scrutiny work in to our programmes. Your letter stated that "the process of developing and finalising Statutory Instruments means it is challenging to share these well in advance of laying." While we appreciate the complexities, the current practices failtomeet the spirit of Lord Parkinson’s commitment. We would appreciate your response to the following points: -What is your target timeline for sharing draft OSA statutory instruments with committees before they are laid? -What is your target timeline for sharing in for mation on relevant OSA draft statements and other documents with committees before they are laid? -What were your reasons for not sharing with the committees in advance copies of the Children’s Codes and revised Statement of Strategic Priorities? Communications and Digital Committee commonssitc@parliament. uk commonssitc@parliament. uk holcommunications@parliament. uk +44 (0)20 719 2793 +44 (0)20 719 8662 holcommunications@parliament. uk +44 (0)20 719 8662 X: @LordsCommsCom We would be grateful if you could reply by 6 June. In light of the public interest in the implementation of the OSA, please note that we intend to place this letter, and your response, in the public domain. Yours sincerely, Chi Onwurah MP Chair – House of Commons Science, Innovation and Technology Committee The Baroness Keeley Chair – House of Lords Communications and Digital Committee
